text: A patient with Wilson's disease has impaired copper metabolism. Which protein is defective, and what is the consequence?
options: Ceruloplasmin synthesis is impaired, causing copper accumulation in blood

ATP7B (copper-transporting ATPase) in hepatocytes is defective, impairing biliary copper excretion — copper accumulates in liver, brain (neuropsychiatric symptoms), and cornea (Kayser-Fleischer rings)
The copper chaperone ATOX1 is deficient, preventing copper delivery to ceruloplasmin
The intestinal copper transporter Ctr1 is overactive, causing excess copper absorption
explanation: Wilson's disease (autosomal recessive) involves mutation of ATP7B, a copper-transporting P-type ATPase in hepatocytes. ATP7B normally exports copper into bile for excretion and incorporates copper into ceruloplasmin. Deficiency leads to copper accumulation: hepatocellular damage → cirrhosis; basal ganglia deposition → tremor, dysarthria; corneal deposition → Kayser-Fleischer rings. Treatment: copper chelation (penicillamine, trientine) or zinc (blocks intestinal absorption).

text: Which type of RNA polymerase transcribes mRNA precursors (pre-mRNA) in eukaryotes?
options: RNA polymerase I
RNA polymerase II
RNA polymerase III
Primase
explanation: RNA Pol II transcribes all protein-coding genes (pre-mRNA), as well as most snRNA and miRNA precursors. RNA Pol I transcribes rRNA (28S, 18S, 5.8S — ~80% of total RNA). RNA Pol III transcribes tRNA, 5S rRNA, and other small RNAs. Alpha-amanitin (from Amanita phalloides mushrooms) potently inhibits RNA Pol II at low concentrations.

text: A mutation in the gene encoding spectrin would most directly affect which cellular structure?
options: Nuclear lamina
The red blood cell cytoskeleton — spectrin forms the flexible network underlying the RBC membrane, maintaining biconcave shape and deformability
Intermediate filaments in neurons
Microtubule organizing center (MTOC)
explanation: Spectrin (α and β subunits form heterodimers) is the major protein of the erythrocyte cytoskeletal network, connected to the plasma membrane via ankyrin (to band 3) and protein 4.1 (to glycophorin C). It provides the structural basis for RBC flexibility and biconcave shape. Mutations cause hereditary spherocytosis (osmotically fragile, spherical RBCs → hemolytic anemia).

text: What is the biochemical basis of sickle cell disease?
options: A frameshift mutation causing premature stop in the β-globin gene
A single missense mutation (Glu6Val) in the β-globin gene — hydrophobic valine replaces glutamate, allowing deoxygenated HbS to polymerize into fibers that distort RBCs into sickle shape
Deletion of the α-globin gene cluster
A nonsense mutation in the γ-globin gene
explanation: SCD is caused by a point mutation (GAG→GTG at codon 6) encoding a glutamate→valine substitution in β-globin. Deoxygenated HbS polymerizes via hydrophobic interactions between the Val6 on one β chain and a hydrophobic pocket on an adjacent HbS molecule. Sickling causes vaso-occlusion, hemolysis, and organ damage. Heterozygotes (HbAS) have relative protection against falciparum malaria.

text: Which of the following best explains why the inner mitochondrial membrane must be impermeable to H+ ions?
options: To prevent H+ from reacting with O2 and forming water prematurely
To maintain the proton gradient (ΔμH+) generated by the electron transport chain — this electrochemical gradient drives H+ back through ATP synthase (Complex V) to synthesize ATP (chemiosmosis)
To prevent ATP from leaking out of the mitochondrial matrix
To allow only NADH to cross the membrane freely
explanation: The ETC pumps H+ from the matrix to the intermembrane space, creating a proton-motive force (PMF) consisting of a chemical gradient (ΔpH) and an electrical gradient (ΔΨ). The inner membrane's impermeability to H+ forces protons to return through ATP synthase's F0 channel, driving rotation of the F1 subunit and ATP synthesis. Uncouplers (e.g., DNP, thermogenin/UCP1) dissipate the gradient, producing heat instead of ATP.

text: A student discovers a new bacterium that lacks a cell wall. This organism would be most similar to which known organisms?
options: Chlamydia (obligate intracellular parasites)
Mycoplasma — bacteria that naturally lack cell walls and are the smallest self-replicating organisms; naturally resistant to penicillins and cephalosporins
Archaea in high-temperature environments
Prions
explanation: Mycoplasma (and related genera like Ureaplasma) are the only bacteria that naturally lack a peptidoglycan cell wall. Their plasma membrane contains sterols for stability (unusual for bacteria). They are naturally resistant to beta-lactam antibiotics and vancomycin (which target cell wall synthesis). Atypical pneumonia caused by M. pneumoniae is treated with macrolides, fluoroquinolones, or tetracyclines.

text: In which phase of mitosis do chromosomes align at the metaphase plate?
options: Prophase
Metaphase
Anaphase
Telophase
explanation: During metaphase, the mitotic spindle (kinetochore microtubules) aligns chromosomes at the cell's equatorial plane (metaphase plate). The spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC) ensures all kinetochores are properly attached to spindle fibers before anaphase onset. In anaphase, sister chromatids are pulled apart to opposite poles (driven by depolymerization of kinetochore microtubules and motor proteins).

text: A cell-surface receptor that, upon ligand binding, dimerizes and autophosphorylates its own tyrosine residues best describes:
options: A G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR)
A receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) — e.g., EGFR, PDGFR, insulin receptor; activation leads to SH2 domain recruitment, Ras activation, and MAPK/PI3K signaling
A nuclear receptor
A cytokine receptor signaling via JAK-STAT
explanation: RTKs are single-pass transmembrane proteins with extracellular ligand-binding domains and intracellular kinase domains. Ligand binding → dimerization → transautophosphorylation of tyrosine residues in the activation loop and on receptor tails. Phosphotyrosines recruit SH2-domain proteins (Grb2, PI3K, PLCγ) → Ras/MAPK, PI3K/Akt cascades. Mutations causing constitutive RTK activity drive many cancers.

text: What is the role of the TATA box in eukaryotic transcription?
options: It is a coding sequence translated into the N-terminal signal peptide
It is a core promoter element recognized by TATA-binding protein (TBP, part of TFIID), positioning RNA polymerase II for accurate transcription initiation approximately 25 bp downstream
It marks the site of 3' polyadenylation
It is the sequence recognized by spliceosomes for intron removal
explanation: The TATA box (consensus TATAAA) is located ~25-30 bp upstream of the transcription start site in many but not all eukaryotic promoters (~24% of human gene promoters). TBP binds the minor groove of the TATA box, severely bending DNA and nucleating the assembly of the pre-initiation complex (PIC) including TFIIA, TFIIB, TFIID, TFIIE, TFIIF, TFIIH, and RNA Pol II.

text: A researcher observes that a tumor cell has lost expression of the gene encoding p16 (CDKN2A/Ink4a). What is the consequence for the cell cycle?
options: Constitutive activation of the p53 pathway

Loss of G1/S checkpoint control — p16 inhibits CDK4/6, which normally phosphorylates Rb to release E2F transcription factors; without p16, CDK4/6 is constitutively active, Rb is hyperphosphorylated, E2F is freed, and cells continuously enter S phase
Cells arrest in G2/M due to unrestricted CDK1 activity
Cells undergo constitutive apoptosis via the intrinsic pathway
explanation: p16 (encoded by CDKN2A) is a CDK inhibitor that blocks CDK4/6 activity. CDK4/6 with cyclin D phosphorylate Rb, releasing E2F to drive S-phase gene expression. Without p16, CDK4/6 activity is unrestricted → Rb hyperphosphorylation → uncontrolled E2F activity → uncontrolled S-phase entry and proliferation. CDKN2A is the second most commonly mutated gene in human cancer (after TP53).

text: The enzyme that catalyzes the first committed step of glycolysis is:
options: Hexokinase (glucose → glucose-6-phosphate)
Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1): fructose-6-phosphate → fructose-1,6-bisphosphate — the rate-limiting, regulated, irreversible committed step
Pyruvate kinase
Phosphoglucose isomerase
explanation: PFK-1 catalyzes the rate-limiting, committed step of glycolysis. It is activated by AMP, ADP, fructose-2,6-bisphosphate (F-2,6-BP; highest-impact activator, produced by PFK-2) and inhibited by ATP and citrate (indicating cellular energy/biosynthetic sufficiency). Hexokinase commits glucose to glycolysis, but is not the rate-limiting step. PFK-1 is the primary regulatory checkpoint.

text: Botulinum toxin causes flaccid paralysis by which mechanism?
options: Blocking nicotinic ACh receptors on skeletal muscle
Cleaving SNARE proteins (SNAP-25 or synaptobrevin/VAMP) required for synaptic vesicle fusion at the neuromuscular junction, preventing ACh release
Irreversibly inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, causing sustained depolarization and receptor desensitization
Blocking voltage-gated Ca2+ channels at the presynaptic terminal
explanation: Botulinum toxin (BoNT, produced by Clostridium botulinum) is a Zn2+-dependent protease that cleaves SNARE complex proteins — SNAP-25 (BoNT/A, E), synaptobrevin/VAMP (BoNT/B, D, F, G), or syntaxin (BoNT/C). SNARE proteins mediate synaptic vesicle fusion with the presynaptic membrane; their cleavage prevents ACh release, causing flaccid paralysis. Tetanus toxin also cleaves SNARE proteins but acts at inhibitory interneurons.

text: Which of the following describes the mechanism by which aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX)?
options: Reversible competitive inhibition — aspirin competes with arachidonic acid at the active site
Irreversible covalent acetylation of a serine residue in the COX active site, permanently blocking arachidonic acid access and inhibiting thromboxane A2 and prostaglandin synthesis
Allosteric inhibition by binding a regulatory site on COX
Irreversible inhibition via zinc chelation in the COX active site
explanation: Aspirin irreversibly acetylates Ser530 in COX-1 and Ser516 in COX-2, blocking the channel through which arachidonic acid accesses the active site. This prevents thromboxane A2 synthesis in platelets (which lack nuclei and cannot synthesize new COX) for the platelet's entire lifespan (~10 days). Other NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) are reversible COX inhibitors.

text: A patient with Lesch-Nyhan syndrome has a deficiency of hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT). What metabolic consequence results?
options: Elevated orotic acid due to pyrimidine overproduction
Elevated uric acid due to failure to salvage hypoxanthine and guanine — purines cannot be recycled and are instead fully degraded to uric acid via xanthine oxidase
Elevated homocysteine due to impaired methionine recycling
Deficient purine de novo synthesis causing anemia
explanation: HGPRT is required for the purine salvage pathway: hypoxanthine + PRPP → IMP, and guanine + PRPP → GMP. Without HGPRT, purines are not recycled; PRPP accumulates and drives increased de novo purine synthesis and degradation, producing excess uric acid. Gout, nephrolithiasis, and characteristic self-mutilation (choreoathetosis, intellectual disability) result. Treatment: allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor).

text: What is the role of the Golgi apparatus in protein processing?
options: Synthesis of all membrane proteins

Processing, sorting, and trafficking of proteins from the ER — includes glycosylation modification (trimming and addition of sugars), proteolytic cleavage, sulfation, and sorting to destinations (lysosomes, plasma membrane, secretion)
Degradation of misfolded proteins via the UPR
ATP-dependent protein folding by chaperones
explanation: Proteins arrive at the Golgi cis face from the ER and traffic through medial to trans Golgi, undergoing sequential modifications: N-glycan remodeling (trimming mannose, adding GlcNAc, galactose, sialic acid), O-glycosylation, sulfation, and phosphorylation. In the trans Golgi network (TGN), proteins are sorted: mannose-6-phosphate tags target proteins to lysosomes; others go to the plasma membrane or are secreted.

text: A patient has elevated serum levels of unconjugated bilirubin and is jaundiced, but liver enzymes and conjugated bilirubin are normal. The most likely cause is:
options: Obstruction of the bile duct

Prehepatic (hemolytic) jaundice — excess RBC breakdown releases unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin faster than the liver can conjugate it; alternatively, impaired conjugation (Gilbert's syndrome: reduced UGT1A1)
Hepatocellular damage impairing all liver functions
Impaired secretion of conjugated bilirubin into bile (Dubin-Johnson syndrome)
explanation: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with normal liver function suggests either overproduction (hemolysis) or impaired conjugation (Gilbert's syndrome: benign UGT1A1 variant; Crigler-Najjar: severe UGT1A1 deficiency). Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia indicates post-hepatic obstruction or impaired secretion. In hepatocellular disease, both forms elevate along with transaminases.

text: Which of the following is true about the SOS response in bacteria?
options: It is an immediate response to oxidative stress mediated by catalase induction

It is an emergency DNA repair system activated when RecA binds ssDNA at stalled replication forks — RecA-ssDNA complex promotes LexA auto-cleavage, derepressing ~40 SOS genes including those for error-prone translesion synthesis polymerases
It activates flagellar motility to escape DNA-damaging environments
It triggers biofilm formation in response to antibiotic treatment
explanation: The SOS response is activated when RecA binds to ssDNA accumulated at stalled replication forks. RecA-ssDNA filament acts as a coprotease, stimulating LexA repressor to cleave itself, derepressing ~40 genes including UvrABC (NER), RecA, sulA (cell division inhibitor), and error-prone polymerases (Pol IV/DinB, Pol V/UmuC-UmuD). Mutagenic SOS repair (translesion synthesis) allows replication past damaged templates at the cost of accuracy.

text: What happens to the free energy change (ΔG) of a biochemical reaction as products accumulate?
options: ΔG becomes more negative, driving the reaction further forward
ΔG becomes less negative (or more positive), because ΔG = ΔG° + RT ln Q, and increasing Q (product/reactant ratio) raises ΔG toward zero (equilibrium)
ΔG remains constant because it is determined solely by ΔG°
ΔG becomes more positive at first, then abruptly becomes negative
explanation: ΔG = ΔG° + RT ln([products]/[reactants]). As products accumulate, the reaction quotient Q increases, making RT ln Q more positive (less negative), and ΔG approaches zero (equilibrium). At equilibrium, ΔG = 0 (no driving force). Cells maintain reactions far from equilibrium by continuously consuming products (coupling reactions) to keep Q << Keq and ΔG negative.

text: A neonate presents with cataracts, jaundice, liver disease, and hypoglycemia after breast feeding. Reducing substances are found in the urine but not glucose. Which enzyme is most likely deficient?
options: Glucose-6-phosphatase (von Gierke disease)
Galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase (GALT) — classic galactosemia; galactose-1-phosphate and galactitol accumulate, causing liver damage, cataracts (galactitol), and intellectual disability
Galactokinase alone (milder, cataracts only)
Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase
explanation: Classic galactosemia (GALT deficiency) causes accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate (toxic to liver, kidney, brain) and galactitol (toxic to lens → cataracts). Galactose (from lactose in breast milk) is a reducing sugar detected by Clinitest (copper reduction) but not by glucose oxidase dipstick. Treatment: eliminate lactose and galactose from diet. Galactokinase deficiency causes only cataracts (galactitol only).

text: In a Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium population, if the frequency of an autosomal recessive disease is 1/10,000, what is the carrier frequency?
options: Approximately 1/50 (2pq ≈ 2q for rare alleles, where q = 1/100 since q² = 1/10,000)
explanation: If q² = 1/10,000, then q = 1/100 (allele frequency). p = 1 − q ≈ 1 (for rare alleles). Carrier frequency = 2pq ≈ 2(1)(1/100) = 1/50. For rare autosomal recessive conditions, there are approximately 2/q × more carriers than affected individuals. This is essential for genetic counseling: a population disease frequency of 1/10,000 means ~2% are carriers.

text: Which organelle is primarily responsible for the degradation of hydrogen peroxide?
options: Lysosomes, using acid hydrolases
Peroxisomes, using catalase (2 H₂O₂ → 2 H₂O + O₂)
Mitochondria, using glutathione peroxidase
The endoplasmic reticulum, using cytochrome P450
explanation: Peroxisomes are organelles that contain catalase, which dismutates H₂O₂ to water and O₂. Peroxisomes also perform fatty acid β-oxidation (of very long chain fatty acids), plasmalogen synthesis, and bile acid synthesis. Zellweger syndrome (absent peroxisomes) causes accumulation of VLCFA and bile acid precursors. Lysosomes use acid hydrolases at pH ~5; mitochondria use glutathione peroxidase and superoxide dismutase.

text: A patient is given a drug that blocks all muscarinic acetylcholine receptors. Which of the following effects would be expected?
options: Bradycardia and increased GI motility
Tachycardia, dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, mydriasis, and decreased secretions — classic anticholinergic (antimuscarinic) effects
Skeletal muscle paralysis
Hypertension from α1-adrenergic receptor activation
explanation: Muscarinic receptors mediate parasympathetic effects: decreased heart rate (M2 in SA node), increased GI motility and secretions (M3), pupillary constriction (M3 in iris sphincter), bladder contraction (M3). Blocking muscarinic receptors with atropine or scopolamine produces the opposite: tachycardia, constipation, urinary retention, mydriasis, dry mouth, anhidrosis. Mnemonic: 'Can't see, can't pee, can't spit, can't sh*t, heart races, skin is dry.'

text: A point mutation changes a codon from UGG (Trp) to UAG. This mutation is best described as a:
options: Missense mutation
Nonsense mutation — UAG is an amber stop codon; premature termination truncates the protein, typically causing loss of function
Silent mutation
Frameshift mutation
explanation: Nonsense mutations convert an amino acid codon to a stop codon (UAA, UAG, UGA), causing premature translation termination. The resulting truncated protein is usually non-functional and often degraded by nonsense-mediated decay (NMD). Missense mutations change one amino acid to another. Silent (synonymous) mutations change a codon but encode the same amino acid. Frameshift mutations alter the reading frame.

text: Which of the following best explains why adipocytes are the primary site of triglyceride storage?
options: Adipocytes express glycogen phosphorylase for efficient energy mobilization

Adipocytes express lipoprotein lipase on their surface (releasing fatty acids from circulating chylomicrons and VLDL), and contain high levels of glycerol-3-phosphate for fatty acid re-esterification, plus lipid droplet-associated proteins (perilipins) for efficient packing
Adipocytes cannot perform fatty acid oxidation, so they have no choice but to store lipids
Adipocytes receive triglycerides directly via gap junctions from hepatocytes
explanation: Adipocytes are specialized for lipid storage: (1) LPL on capillary surfaces cleaves TG in lipoproteins, releasing fatty acids for uptake; (2) insulin stimulates glucose uptake (GLUT4) to generate glycerol-3-phosphate for esterification; (3) perilipins coat lipid droplets, regulating both storage and lipolysis; (4) HSL (hormone-sensitive lipase) is activated by catecholamines/glucagon during fasting for TG mobilization.

text: A researcher blocks the action of GTP-binding protein Gαi. What would be the expected effect on cAMP levels in cells stimulated by an opioid receptor agonist?
options: cAMP levels would decrease further, because Gαi stimulates phosphodiesterase
cAMP levels would not decrease (or would be higher than expected) — Gαi normally inhibits adenylyl cyclase; blocking Gαi removes this inhibition, preventing the opioid-mediated decrease in cAMP
cAMP levels would increase due to direct activation of adenylyl cyclase by the blocked Gαi
cAMP levels would be unaffected because opioid receptors do not use G proteins
explanation: Opioid receptors (μ, δ, κ) are Gi-coupled GPCRs. Activation → Gαi inhibits adenylyl cyclase → decreased cAMP → decreased PKA activity → reduced neuronal excitability (analgesia). Blocking Gαi prevents this inhibition; adenylyl cyclase remains active; cAMP levels do not fall as expected. This is relevant to opioid tolerance: chronic opioid exposure causes compensatory upregulation of adenylyl cyclase.
